methodology_sections: 1. Lifecycle Integrity - State transitions, illegal states, predecessors
2. Monotonicity & Irreversibility - Increasing values, one-way transitions
3. Access & Permission Logic - Conditional access, role requirements
4. Time & Resource Integrity - Time-locks, resource bounds, expiry

invariants.description: initializeAlphaIndices() has onlyOwner modifier, but TraitForgeNft._incrementGeneration() calls it. TraitForgeNft is set as allowedCaller, not owner. Generation increment will always revert, permanently locking the protocol at generation 1.
invariants.attack_scenario: After 10,000 tokens are minted in generation 1, _incrementGeneration is called which invokes entropyGenerator.initializeAlphaIndices(). This reverts because msg.sender (TraitForgeNft) != owner (deployer). No more tokens can ever be minted via mintToken or mintWithBudget since _mintInternal calls _incrementGeneration. The entire protocol freezes.
invariants.invariant_logic: TraitForgeNft must be authorized to call initializeAlphaIndices: (msg.sender == allowedCaller || msg.sender == owner) for initializeAlphaIndices
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_genIncrementCallable() public { // After generationMintCounts[1] >= maxTokensPerGen, _incrementGeneration must not revert // assert(entropyGenerator.owner() == address(nftContract) || initializeAlphaIndices has onlyAllowedCaller); }

invariants.description: mintWithBudget uses `_tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen` as loop condition. _tokenIds is a global counter across all generations, while maxTokensPerGen is 10,000. After generation 1 completes, _tokenIds >= 10000, so the loop condition is always false and mintWithBudget becomes permanently non-functional.
invariants.attack_scenario: After generation 1 is fully minted (10000 tokens), any user calling mintWithBudget for generation 2+ will have their entire budget refunded because the while loop never executes (_tokenIds=10000 is not < maxTokensPerGen=10000). Users can only mint one at a time via mintToken, which may not be the intended design.
invariants.invariant_logic: mintWithBudget loop condition should use generationMintCounts[currentGeneration] < maxTokensPerGen, not _tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_mintWithBudgetWorksAllGens() public view { // For any generation g > 1 where generationMintCounts[g] < maxTokensPerGen, // mintWithBudget should be able to mint tokens assert(true); // Cannot express as on-chain invariant }

invariants.description: All contracts inherit Pausable and use whenNotPaused modifiers, but none expose external pause() or unpause() functions. The _pause() and _unpause() are internal in OpenZeppelin's Pausable. Since no contract defines public/external wrappers, the pause mechanism is completely non-functional—the protocol can never be paused despite having pause checks throughout.
invariants.attack_scenario: If a critical vulnerability is discovered (e.g., fund drainage in NukeFund), the owner cannot pause the contracts. All whenNotPaused checks are effectively dead code. Attackers can continue exploiting even after the team identifies the issue. This affects TraitForgeNft, EntityForging, EntityTrading, NukeFund, DevFund, and EntropyGenerator.
invariants.invariant_logic: For each contract inheriting Pausable and using whenNotPaused: there must exist an external/public function that calls _pause() restricted to onlyOwner
invariants.solidity_property: // No function exists matching: function pause() external onlyOwner { _pause(); } // in any of the contracts

invariants.description: writeEntropyBatch1(), writeEntropyBatch2(), and writeEntropyBatch3() have no access control. Anyone can call them and the entropy is derived from block.number and index, making it fully predictable and front-runnable. A miner/validator or MEV bot can choose exactly when to write batches to control all future token entropies.
invariants.attack_scenario: An attacker monitors the mempool. Before writeEntropyBatch1 is called, they simulate the entropy values that would be generated at the current block.number. They can front-run or back-run the call, or call it themselves at a favorable block, to ensure high-value entropy positions (e.g., 999999 golden god, or high nuke factors) land on tokens they plan to mint. Since entropy determines forge potential, nuke factor, and role, this controls all game outcomes.
invariants.invariant_logic: writeEntropyBatch1/2/3 should require onlyOwner or onlyAllowedCaller AND entropy should use a commit-reveal or VRF scheme
invariants.solidity_property: // assert(msg.sender == owner() || msg.sender == allowedCaller) in writeEntropyBatch1/2/3

invariants.description: listForForging checks `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential` (inclusive), then forgeWithListed increments forgingCounts without re-checking against forgePotential. This off-by-one allows one extra forge beyond the intended limit. A forger with forgePotential=N can forge N+1 times.
invariants.attack_scenario: Token has forgePotential=1. forgingCounts=0: lists (0<=1 passes), forges -> count=1. forgingCounts=1: lists (1<=1 passes), forges -> count=2. The entity forged twice despite forgePotential=1. For the merger side, the check is `forgingCounts[mergerTokenId] <= mergerForgePotential` AFTER increment, so it correctly limits. But the forger listing check is before increment and uses <=, allowing the extra forge.
invariants.invariant_logic: For any forger token: forgingCounts[tokenId] should never exceed forgePotential. Check should be forgingCounts[tokenId] < forgePotential (strict less than)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_forgeLimit(uint256 tokenId) public view { uint256 entropy = nftContract.getTokenEntropy(tokenId); uint8 forgePotential = uint8((entropy / 10) % 10); assert(forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential); }

invariants.description: getNextEntropy wraps currentSlotIndex back to 0 when it reaches maxSlotIndex-1 (769). This means after 10,010 entropy values are consumed (770 slots × 13 values), the same entropy values are reused. Combined with the fact that generation increments reset alpha indices but NOT entropy slot data, tokens across generations can have identical (and predictable) entropies.
invariants.attack_scenario: In generation 2+, the entropy sequence repeats from slot 0. Attackers who observed generation 1 entropies know exactly what entropy each subsequent mint will receive. They can time their mints to obtain tokens with the highest nuke factors or specific forge potentials, extracting maximum value from the NukeFund.
invariants.invariant_logic: Each generation should use fresh entropy: currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex should only wrap after all unique entropies are consumed AND entropy data should be regenerated per generation
invariants.solidity_property: // assert(no two tokens across different generations share entropy from the same slot/number position without re-randomization)

invariants.description: calculateNukeFactor can return a value exceeding MAX_DENOMINATOR as adjustedAge grows over time (unbounded). When finalNukeFactor > nukeFactorMaxParam, the cap (fund/maxAllowedClaimDivisor) applies. However, when finalNukeFactor is between nukeFactorMaxParam and MAX_DENOMINATOR, potentialClaimAmount could exceed maxAllowedClaimAmount without the cap being applied, draining more than the intended max share.
invariants.attack_scenario: If nukeFactorMaxParam is increased above 50000 by admin (or even at default when finalNukeFactor is between 50000 and 100000), potentialClaimAmount = fund * finalNukeFactor / 100000 could be 50-99% of the fund in a single nuke. Since there's no invariant ensuring potentialClaimAmount <= maxAllowedClaimAmount when the cap condition isn't triggered, a patient holder with high-entropy old token can drain a disproportionate share of the fund.
invariants.invariant_logic: claimAmount must always be <= fund / maxAllowedClaimDivisor; equivalently: claimAmount = min(potentialClaimAmount, maxAllowedClaimAmount)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_nukeClaim(uint256 tokenId) public view { uint256 finalNukeFactor = calculateNukeFactor(tokenId); uint256 potentialClaim = (fund * finalNukeFactor) / MAX_DENOMINATOR; uint256 maxClaim = fund / maxAllowedClaimDivisor; // The current code only caps when finalNukeFactor > nukeFactorMaxParam // assert(potentialClaim <= maxClaim || finalNukeFactor > nukeFactorMaxParam); }

invariants.description: _cancelListingForForging deletes the listing entry (listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]]) but does NOT clear listedTokenIds[tokenId]. This leaves a stale pointer. When the token is re-listed, a new listingCount entry is created, but the old listedTokenIds value persists until overwritten. The stale mapping can cause incorrect lookup results during the window between cancel and re-list.
invariants.attack_scenario: Token A is listed (listingCount=1, listedTokenIds[A]=1). The listing is cancelled: listings[1] is deleted but listedTokenIds[A] still equals 1. If another token B is then listed at listingCount=2, and someone queries listings[listedTokenIds[A]], they get the deleted (zeroed) listings[1], not listings[2]. This could bypass the 'already listed' check since _listingInfo.isListed would be false. Additionally, _beforeTokenTransfer checks listedId>0 using the stale value, potentially causing unexpected cancellation attempts.
invariants.invariant_logic: After cancellation: listedTokenIds[tokenId] == 0 AND listings[oldIndex].isListed == false
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_listingConsistency(uint256 tokenId) public view { uint256 idx = listedTokenIds[tokenId]; if (idx > 0) { assert(listings[idx].isListed == true && listings[idx].tokenId == tokenId); } }

invariants.description: In forgeWithListed, the forger's forgingCount is incremented without re-checking against forgePotential. The comment says 'already checked in listForForging', but _resetForgingCountIfNeeded is called between listing and forging, potentially resetting the count. After reset, the forger effectively gets a full new set of forges without the listing's original check being meaningful. Conversely, if the forger was at the limit when listed, and a year passes before someone calls forgeWithListed, the reset restores the count but the listing remains valid from the stale check.
invariants.attack_scenario: Forger with forgePotential=1 and forgingCounts=1 cannot list (1<=1 passes due to off-by-one, but imagine it's fixed to <). However, at the current code: list at count=0, wait 1 year. forgeWithListed calls _resetForgingCountIfNeeded, resetting count to 0. Then increments to 1. The forger effectively got an extra forge because the listing check was done a year ago and the state has changed.
invariants.invariant_logic: forgePotential check must be validated at forging time, not just at listing time: forgingCounts[forgerTokenId] < forgePotential at the moment of forging
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_forgerValidAtForgeTime(uint256 forgerTokenId) public view { uint256 entropy = nftContract.getTokenEntropy(forgerTokenId); uint8 forgePotential = uint8((entropy / 10) % 10); assert(forgingCounts[forgerTokenId] < forgePotential); }

invariants.description: buyNFT does not check listing.isActive. It only verifies listing.seller != address(0). While delete zeros all fields (including isActive=false and seller=address(0)), if the listing data were manipulated or if there's a state inconsistency, the isActive flag provides an independent safety check. More importantly, listNFTForSale sets isActive=true but buyNFT never validates it.
invariants.attack_scenario: If a future code change or upgrade introduces a path where seller is set but isActive is false (e.g., partial cancellation), buyNFT would still process the purchase. Without the isActive check, the contract relies entirely on seller!=address(0) as the validity gate, reducing defense-in-depth.
invariants.invariant_logic: buyNFT should require listing.isActive == true AND listing.seller != address(0)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_buyRequiresActive(uint256 tokenId) public view { Listing memory listing = listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]]; // If a purchase is processing: assert(listing.isActive && listing.seller != address(0)); }

invariants.description: ageMultiplier is initialized to 0 (default) in the constructor and has no initial value set. calculateAge multiplies by ageMultiplier, so until setAgeMultplier is called, all age calculations return 0. This means nuke factor only depends on initialNukeFactor (entropy/40), completely negating the age-based nuke value accrual mechanism. Early nukers get a fixed payout regardless of hold time.
invariants.attack_scenario: After deployment, before setAgeMultplier is called, users can nuke tokens immediately after the minimumDaysHeld period since age contributes 0 to nuke factor. The nuke value is determined entirely by entropy, not by how long the token was held. This undermines the core hold-to-earn mechanic. If the admin never sets ageMultiplier, the time-based value accrual never works.
invariants.invariant_logic: ageMultiplier must be > 0 for calculateAge to return meaningful values; should be set in constructor or enforced > 0

invariants.description: forgeWithListed requires msg.value >= forgingFee but does not refund excess ETH. Any ETH sent above the forging fee is permanently locked in the EntityForging contract with no withdrawal mechanism.
invariants.attack_scenario: A user calls forgeWithListed with msg.value = 1 ETH when forgingFee = 0.1 ETH. 0.01 ETH goes to NukeFund, 0.09 ETH goes to forger owner, and 0.9 ETH remains stuck in the EntityForging contract forever. There is no sweep or withdraw function to recover these funds.
invariants.invariant_logic: For any external payable function: msg.value - consumed_amount must be refunded to msg.sender, OR msg.value must equal exact required amount

invariants.description: currentGeneration should strictly increase and never exceed maxGeneration. The _incrementGeneration function does not check currentGeneration < maxGeneration before incrementing. While forge() checks newGeneration <= maxGeneration, _incrementGeneration (triggered by minting) could push currentGeneration beyond maxGeneration.
invariants.attack_scenario: If currentGeneration equals maxGeneration (10) and maxTokensPerGen tokens are minted in that generation, _incrementGeneration would set currentGeneration to 11, which exceeds maxGeneration. Subsequent mints in generation 11 would succeed since _mintInternal only calls _incrementGeneration when the limit is reached, not before minting. This violates the max generation cap for minted tokens.
invariants.invariant_logic: currentGeneration <= maxGeneration must hold at all times; _incrementGeneration should require currentGeneration < maxGeneration
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_generationBound() public view { assert(currentGeneration <= maxGeneration); }

invariants.description: NukeFund.receive() uses taxCut as a divisor (devShare = msg.value / taxCut). If owner sets taxCut to 0 via setTaxCut, all ETH sent to NukeFund (from minting, trading, forging) will cause a division-by-zero revert, bricking the entire protocol's fund distribution.
invariants.attack_scenario: Owner accidentally (or maliciously) calls setTaxCut(0). Now every mint, trade, and forge that sends ETH to NukeFund reverts because receive() divides by zero. The entire protocol halts: no minting, no trading, no forging. Similarly, EntityForging.forgeWithListed divides by taxCut for devFee calculation.
invariants.invariant_logic: taxCut must be > 0 in all contracts that use it as a divisor; setTaxCut should enforce _taxCut > 0
